raw_response:
C is correct because if it were autosomal or mitochondrial, both males and females would show transmission to offspring regardless of their sex. If it was x-linked recessive, then we'd see more commonly that only males transmit unaffected (due to hemizygosity). Therefore, this pattern indicates maternal transmission which aligns with X-linked dominant inheritance where an affected female will pass on her single copy of the mutation to all daughters (who may express the phenotype depending on penetrance), while sons receive one X from each parent - leading to either complete dominance (if they inherit the mutated gene from the mother) or no expression due to lack of second mutant allele (from father).
The type of inheritance for the given scenario is **C: X-linked dominant**. This conclusion stems from the fact that affected females consistently transmit the condition to their daughters, as seen in X-linked dominant traits, whereas males do not typically pass along such conditions unless inherited directly through their mother's X chromosome.
